_note: Public intellectual-history lineage nodes. Each tradition shows what it contributes to the dissertation argument, which friction dimensions and media-ecology stages it informs, and the public reference ids that belong to it.

traditions.name: Phenomenology and the Lifeworld
traditions.contribution: Names the crisis that follows when formalized systems detach from lived, first-person experience. Grounds the dissertation's concept of existential abstraction.

traditions.name: Learning Sciences of Productive Struggle
traditions.contribution: Supplies the empirical engine: desirable difficulty, productive failure, and retrieval practice show that durable learning requires the very effort that frictionless AI removes.

traditions.name: Canadian Media Theory
traditions.contribution: Establishes that media carry biases of time and space and that the medium reshapes perception. The upstream source for media ecology and for reading AI as an environment, not a tool.

traditions.name: Critical Theory of Technology
traditions.contribution: Argues that technical systems encode values and tend toward efficiency at the expense of judgment. Frames pedagogical friction as a deliberate, democratic intervention.

traditions.name: Constructionism
traditions.contribution: Holds that powerful ideas are built through making and debugging. A direct counterweight to answer-generation: the struggle is where the learning lives.

traditions.name: Media Ecology (Ong)
traditions.contribution: The conceptual spine. Ong's account of how writing and print restructure consciousness is extended here past secondary orality into algorithmic secondary orality and tertiary algorithmicity.

traditions.name: Media Archaeology
traditions.contribution: Reads media materially and across deep time, privileging circuits and code over content. Situates AI within long genealogies rather than as a clean rupture.

traditions.name: Stieglerian Critique
traditions.contribution: Diagnoses cognitive proletarianization: the externalization of memory and skill to systems. The case for reclaiming cognitive craft.

traditions.name: Critical Algorithm Studies
traditions.contribution: Shows how curation and personalization quietly govern what reaches whom. Anchors algorithmic secondary orality and the infrastructural dimension of friction.

traditions.name: Attention, Deep Reading, and Embodiment
traditions.contribution: Documents how digital and algorithmic environments erode deep reading, attention, resilience, and authentic dialogue. The human stakes of frictionless production.

traditions.name: Surveillance Capitalism and Platform Power
traditions.contribution: Names the political economy behind algorithmic systems: data extraction, behavioral prediction, and rent. Locates friction as the defense of cognitive sovereignty.

traditions.name: Postdigital and Critical Literacies
traditions.contribution: Treats the analog and digital as entangled and reframes literacy as critical, creative, and justice-oriented. Supports the productive-versus-exclusionary friction distinction.

traditions.name: Generative AI in Education (Current Scholarship)
traditions.contribution: The contemporary empirical base: teacher perceptions, integration levels, assessment redesign, and AI scaffolding. The terrain the dissertation studies directly.

traditions.name: Interpretive Qualitative Case Study
traditions.contribution: Supplies the methodological anchor: a bounded, interpretive, constructivist case study in Merriam's tradition, within which the qualitative-dominant convergent mixed methods design examines educator and institutional sensemaking.
